does: This paper introduces RLCD, a method for aligning language models to adhere to natural language principles without human feedback. The core technique creates preference training pairs by sampling model outputs under two contrasting prompt conditions: a positive prompt encouraging adherence to desired principles and a negative prompt encouraging violations. These contrastive outputs generate cleaner preference labels than standard approaches by producing more differentiated model behaviors. The method trains a preference model on these synthetic pairs and then uses reinforcement learning with this model as a reward signal to improve a base language model. Empirical evaluation across harmlessness, helpfulness, and creative writing tasks demonstrates improvements over RLAIF and context distillation baselines at both 7B and 30B model scales.
connects: RLCD builds on the emerging paradigm of learning from model-generated synthetic feedback rather than human annotations, extending prior work on RLAIF and context distillation that address the scalability limitations of human-in-the-loop alignment. The approach directly responds to challenges in obtaining clean preference labels when using only language models as annotators by introducing contrastive prompt engineering to increase output differentiation. This connects to broader research on prompt-based conditioning, constitutional AI principles, and reinforcement learning from preference models. The work leverages established techniques in preference modeling and RL-based fine-tuning while innovating on the preference generation stage, positioning itself within the trajectory of research reducing human annotation requirements for alignment.
enables: This work opens several avenues for future research in scalable alignment. The success of contrastive prompt-based preference generation suggests deeper investigation into prompt design and engineering as a lever for alignment without human feedback. The method enables scaling alignment efforts to resource-constrained settings where human annotation is prohibitively expensive. Future work could explore automated discovery of effective positive and negative prompts, application to additional principles beyond harmlessness and helpfulness, and theoretical understanding of when contrastive distillation produces reliable preference signals. The approach also invites research into combining synthetic preference learning with other alignment techniques, investigating failure modes where negative prompts fail to reliably induce principle violations, and extending the framework to multi-objective alignment scenarios.
